Data.MeaningHint: What are the images of, anyway? Well, it's better not to say out loud. Just think of the kind of image you enjoy looking at most. That's what's in the box at the end of the street. Aren't you the lucky one?
Data.ReadingHint: Try to see the images of the gargoyles. Winged, stony monsters. Very cool. And the roosters? Colorful little dinosaurs. What's not to like?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Okay, I didn't want to tell you this, but the <kanji>image</kanji>s are all <kanji>drawing</kanji>s and <kanji>painting</kanji>s of <reading>ga</reading>rgoyles (が) and <reading>cock</reading>s (かく). Male chickens, that is. Turns out that's what you wanted to see most. Don't worry, no one is judging you.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You go down a <radical>street</radical> that cuts through a <radical>rice paddy</radical> and leads to a <radical>box</radical>. When you open the box at the end of the street, you see it's full of <kanji>image</kanji>s. Image after image. <kanji>Drawing</kanji>, <kanji>painting</kanji>, you name it. Someone must have left this box of images here.

Data.MeaningHint: If you keep up with your radicals, your kanji studies will never go south. Haha! Go south! Get it? I said g—
Data.ReadingHint: Picture Mean Amy down in the south, saying mean things in her American southern accent: "Y'all don't come back now, ya hear?" Man, Mean Amy is so mean. And southern.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Think about the <kanji>south</kanji>. What's down there? <reading>Mean Amy</reading> (みなみ) lives down there. Think of the last mean thing she did to you and imagine it while yelling out "MEAN AMYYYYY" at the sky.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You've seen this as a radical before, and the kanji means the same thing — <kanji>south</kanji>!

Data.MeaningHint: If you know the radical you know this.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine having to use regular, ordinary ketchup, or just old, boring catsup on your burger. Or even worse, an actual tomato. BLEH!
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
To remember けつ, we use the word "ketsup." It's the perfect blend of ketchup and catsup!

You're <kanji>lack</kanji>ing something in your fridge. You look around, trying to find the <reading>ketsu</reading>p (けつ) you lack. You can't find it, because you lack it.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: This kanji is the same as the radical it comes from. <radical>Lack</radical> becomes <kanji>Lack</kanji>!

Data.MeaningHint: Think about your own surname in your language. Where did it come from? Were you a clan of village dwellers? Maybe you made shoes! You should look it up and find out!
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine your ancestors, herding sheep. Thanks to them your name means "sheep people." Thanks, great-great-great-great-great-grandpappy.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Don't worry, I'll tell you where your <kanji>family name</kanji> came from. Your family used to herd <reading>shee</reading>p (し). You read that right, your family was of the sheep clan because you took care of sheep all day.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>clan</radical> is where you get your name. But not your given name, your <kanji>family name</kanji>. That's why people have names that mean "in a rice paddy" and "near the ferry crossing." The location of your clan determined your <kanji>surname</kanji>! Hope your ancestors didn't live on "Poo Lake" or something.

Data.MeaningHint: Because it's a cross with a mouth, it thinks that it is reason in all the chaos.
Data.ReadingHint: "Those youths just do things for no reason! That's why I, the Cross of Reason, am here! I will knock some reason into those youths."
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>reason</kanji> that this Cross of Reason exists is to combat the <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) of the world, who just bring chaos into everything.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A giant <radical>cross</radical> with a <radical>mouth</radical> stands above you. "I am <kanji>reason</kanji>!" it says to you. It is the Cross of Reason.

Data.MeaningHint: The sick part is that all those bills were once this tree's friends. Picture the tree sobbing uncontrollably, as pieces of its former friends fall everywhere around it. No wonder it needs that umbrella.
Data.ReadingHint: Try to remember this little girl named Satsuki, since she'll come up whenever the reading for a kanji is さつ (there's a good handful of them). Picture her in your mind, with details like her hair and clothes. Maybe even holding an umbrella or standing by a tree? You should also know she's kind of a bookworm, I guess, in terms of personality. If you can imagine her clearly now, it'll be easy to remember さつ kanji later on.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: And the person who gave the tree an umbrella to protect it from all those <kanji>bills</kanji>? <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ), a little girl famous for… handing out umbrellas, I guess. Makes sense that Satsuki would take care of these bills then.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>tree</radical> has an <radical>umbrella</radical>, which it's holding over its head. Strange, because trees normally like water… Turns out, this tree wasn't getting showered in water, but in dollar <kanji>bill</kanji>s instead.

Data.MeaningHint: The mouth clan are distinct peoples. They do, of course, use their mouths.
Data.ReadingHint: That's why they're the mouth clan. This nation of people are known for mouthing off and saying mean things. Imagine them, as a nation, saying mean things to you. How awful! All being from the same place, they're all into it too. In a way, they're not the mouth clan, they're the mean clan.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The mouth clan <kanji>peoples</kanji> are a <kanji>nation</kanji> of <kanji>people</kanji> who use their mouths to say <reading>mean</reading> (みん) things to other peoples.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>mouth</radical> <radical>clan</radical> are a group of <kanji>people</kanji>. They are the <kanji>people</kanji> of a <kanji>nation</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine riding your scooter around in a really large area, pressing your sword into the ground as you do. Once you've finished carving out the sides of the entire area, you claim to everyone around that this is YOUR area, and they aren't allowed in.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine doing everything you can to get this hen out of your area, but it manages to dodge and outwit you at every turn. You even slash at it with your sword and try to run it over with your scooter, but the hen can't be stopped. This is the hen's area now. You have been defeated.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you look down at your <kanji>area</kanji> with its sword-carved <kanji>side</kanji>s, you suddenly realize there is a <reading>hen</reading> (へん) pecking at the ground in there. "Um, excuse me, hen, I believe I said this is MY area?!" You try to kick it out, but it hops over your leg.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: While riding your <radical>scooter</radical>, you take your <radical>sword</radical> and cut out an <kanji>area</kanji> on the ground.  You carefully cut all the <kanji>side</kanji>s as you go. This area's all yours now!

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine guiding your leader's dainty feet into the heeled shoes, then attaching the weird head replica. Now picture yourself trying to keep a straight face as you tell them they totally look tall enough.
Data.ReadingHint: Just focus on how attaching requires two things to remember this reading.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You have to remember the kun'yomi reading of this kanji, and the part you need to remember is つ. So, think of how you would <kanji>attach</kanji> something to something else. What would you need? You'd need <reading>two</reading> (つ) things to put together!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: When you take your <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>measurement</radical>s, you realize they're too short, so you <kanji>attach</kanji> things to them to make the measurements more impressive. Attach some heels to the shoes, then attach another head on top of theirs. There — perfect measurements thanks to these attachments.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture the person, the lion is leaning over them, the drop of spit dangling from their mouth. "I-I want to go back where I came from," the person gasps, shaking.
Data.ReadingHint: The eagle came from the sky and technically rescued him, but compared to where he was before, it may not be a better situation. I think this person is going to be eaten either way.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>From</kanji> above there is a loud caw, and a giant <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) swoops down, forcing the lion to back off. The eagle grabs the person with its talons, bringing him into the air! But now he's... in the air being carried by a giant eagle, <kanji>compared with</kanji> where he was before, is this really better?!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>lion</radical> drops a <radical>drop</radical> of saliva onto this <radical>person</radical> <kanji>from</kanji> above. The person is in a much worse place <kanji>compared with</kanji> where they were before, <kanji>since</kanji> they only just recently fell into this pit.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine looking out the window of your house and watching your husband slide down his slide over and over. He's not even good at it, he falls off the side halfway down most of the time. You want to complain, but he's pretty decent otherwise. This is his only fault.
Data.ReadingHint: You run outside and yell, "AHA! It's your fault that shih tzu died! You and that stupid slide!"
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You try to come up with another <kanji>fault</kanji> of his so you can complain, when you see a tiny <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ) trotting by. Oh no! Before you can call out, your husband has fallen off the slide and crushed the poor thing. You hear a sharp yelp and then nothing.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: That <radical>slide</radical> is your <radical>husband</radical>'s only <kanji>fault</kanji>. He loves that slide, more than you, and it's the only thing you don't like about him.

Data.MeaningHint: Implanting a slide into your heart is certain to improve your cardiovascular health.
Data.ReadingHint: Feel the impact as the doctor hits you again and again. You're not certain that this is safe, but you do feel certain you've got a terrible doctor.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: To be <kanji>certain</kanji> that the heart-slide is working, the doctor who put it there <reading>hits</reading> (ひつ) you in the chest a few times as a test.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your heart has a slide running through it because you want to be certain that the blood is flowing in and out of it smoothly. Given how important this is, you want to be absolutely certain.

Data.MeaningHint: Feel the irritation of this. How irritating is this kid going to be the whole flight over? The jet is going as fast as it can!
Data.ReadingHint: Go ahead and pretend to listen to the kid asking if we're there yet. Then in your mind go "me me me me me me me" quietly.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: What a selfish kid, right? Well, all he thinks about is "<reading>Me</reading> (み) me me me me me me me me me me." Hate kids like that.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You're flying in a <radical>jet</radical>. Even though it's one of the fastest things on earth, your child is sitting in the back asking, "Are we there yet? Are we there yet?" You have to keep responding. "<kanji>Not yet</kanji>! Not yet! NOT YET!"

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the end of the earth being like how they imagined it pre-Columbus era. Just a big drop going off into space, giant waterfalls plummeting down. Your crazy jet has made it to the very end! Congratulations!
Data.ReadingHint: It's surprisingly dirty down here, because all the water takes the garbage this way. That's why you sit on these mats you brought.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: When you get to the <kanji>end</kanji> of the world, you land your jet and put out a few <reading>mats</reading> (まつ) to sit on so you can view the sights.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: This kanji uses a modified <radical>jet</radical> radical. The front area of the wing is wider than the middle area of the wing. This may seem counterintuitive, but by doing this they can fit so much more jet fuel. The jet zooms off and keeps going for a long, long time, until it reaches the <kanji>end</kanji> of the earth.

Data.MeaningHint: He goes on to explain that he changed your school to here because there is a tree to block the satellite's view. Also, he wears a tin foil lid on his head so they can't track him. The regular school was no good because "they were watching." So now you have this new school, taught under a tree by your father who's wearing a lid on his head.
Data.ReadingHint: On your first day of school, imagine walking under the tree and seeing all your classmates. They're all こういち–similar to each other, but with some variation. Your father explains these こういちs escaped from a government research facility, and they come to this school to receive a better education.  "These poor こういちs need a school that will teach them more than just kanji and Japanese vocabulary."
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Your new <kanji>school</kanji> has other students too. They are all <reading>こう</reading>いちs, though.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Next to the <radical>tree</radical>, a <radical>lid</radical> is put on your <radical>father</radical>. "Hello child," he says. "This is your new <kanji>school</kanji>."

Data.MeaningHint: Just think of the eye leaf as weird (it is weird, after all). It grows in the winter. Now, think of any normal leaves. They do the exact opposite, which is to grow in the summer.
Data.ReadingHint: Think about summer. Now think about yourself tying knots. Now think about summer again. Now think about tying knots again. Now think about your mother getting upset at you because all you do is tie knots all summer long. Go play video games like a normal kid!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Now it's summertime! What do you do during the <kanji>summer</kanji>? You just sit around with your favorite string tying <reading>knots</reading> (なつ) in it over and over again.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leaf</radical> shaped like an <radical>eye</radical> only grows in <radical>winter</radical>. Every other leaf, however, will do the opposite, which is to grow in the <kanji>summer</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Just look at these weak, iced-over bows. If you tried to draw back their strings they'd snap into a million pieces.
Data.ReadingHint: Now you're stuck with two weak broken bows and no jack. Ice is the worst.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You take the bows out to your car, which is stuck in a bunch of ice and snow, and try to use them as a kind of <kanji>weak</kanji> <reading>jack</reading> (じゃく). You know, to get your car unstuck. Unfortunately, your bows are too weak and brittle to use as jacks, and they just snap in half.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You have two <radical>bow</radical>s but they're both covered in <radical>ice</radical>. This has made them very <kanji>weak</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine being in a temple and realizing you don't know what time it is. Never fear! Look on up at the sun. Ah, yes. Blinding noon.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine him going to all kinds of different times and also make sure you're specifically imagining Jesus doing all these things. Put these two together and you'll remember the reading in no time. (Haha, get it?)
Data.ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Time</kanji> in a temple makes you think of <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). Specifically, Jesus being able to perform time travel magic. He winks at you, goes back to the dinosaur ages, where he wrestles a T-rex. Then, he goes to WWII era and punches Hitler in the mouth.
Data.MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>sun</radical> is used in a <radical>temple</radical> to tell <kanji>time</kanji>. They don't have clocks or cell phones, they have sun dials and good old fashioned "looking up and seeing where the sun is right now."

This also can mean <kanji>o' clock</kanji>, as in "five o'clock" It's used when measuring what time it is in Japanese.

Data.MeaningHint: The paper itself was also made by your clan using thread woven really tightly together.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine a big guard coming up to you and asking to see your papers so they can see what clan you belong to. Well you're from the Commi clan, so you really hope this guard is okay with that.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You need this specific <kanji>paper</kanji> to prove that you aren't a <reading>Commi</reading> (かみ), aka a Communist. Or that you are. It really depends on where you want to go and what decade this is.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> that ties you to your <radical>clan</radical> is <kanji>paper</kanji>. Your papers, that is.

Data.MeaningHint:
Just imagine writing in a diary and saying everything to write aloud to yourself in your head. 

"Dear me, today I learned a lot of new kanji on WaniKani. It was the best. I love the crabigator sooooo much. Love, me."
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the key you use to keep what you write down a secret. What does it look like? How does it feel in your hands?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You need to protect what you <kanji>write down</kanji> and <kanji>record</kanji> for yourself with a <reading>key</reading> (き). These are your special thoughts and feelings, you don't want anyone else to see! Lock them away with a key!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> to <radical>oneself</radical> (yourself), you decide to <kanji>write down</kanji>. Whether this is in the form of a journal, diary, or blog, you feel the need to <kanji>record</kanji> these important thoughts in some way.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine your mama passing through your house on a scooter, not stopping because she needs to get her task done urgently.
Data.ReadingHint: Why do you have to do the task too? Because there are two tasks, so the two of you have to do them.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you watch your mama <kanji>pass through</kanji> the house, she yells, "YOU HAVE TO DO THIS TASK <reading>TOO</reading> (つう)!" Too?! Oh no, now you have to do it too. Hurry up!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>scooter</radical> with your <radical>mama</radical> carrying a <radical>task</radical> she needs to get done. You see her <kanji>pass through</kanji>, hurrying by to get that task done.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine yourself saying how tall and expensive you are to everyone in the room. Then they use a lid to cover your mouth so you can't talk to anyone anymore. But, then your mustache starts talking, saying the same thing. That's why this lid covers both your mustache and your mouth. Now you can't say that you are tall or expensive.
Data.ReadingHint: The こういちs finally get tired of your shenanigans, and lid up your mouth and mustache so you can't say that you're tall or expensive.
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
"I am <kanji>tall</kanji>er and more <kanji>expensive</kanji> than you." 

You're saying this because there are some <reading>こう</reading>いちs in the room, and you want to make them feel inferior (you're a こういち hater). You keep comparing your height to the こういちs in the room, and you keep checking price tags. Then you tell them you're taller and more expensive than they are.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>lid</radical> on your <radical>mouth</radical> and <radical>mustache</radical>, covering up all your talking holes. This is because you kept saying how <kanji>tall</kanji> and <kanji>expensive</kanji> you are.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine holding this bow. Feel how strong it is as you draw the string. Now look closely at the insect bodies it's made of. Thanks for your help, little guys.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine all the people who live in きょうと, using their strong arms to make strong bows out of strong insect carapaces. Could きょうと be Japan's strongest city?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You had this <kanji>strong</kanji> bow made especially for you in <reading>きょう</reading>と. きょうと is famous for their strong bows, so if you ever want another, you know where to go.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You keep your <radical>bow</radical> <radical>private</radical> because it's made of <radical>insect</radical>s, meaning it's extremely <kanji>strong</kanji>. Insect bodies are really durable, so they make for super strong bows. That's why you have to keep this one private — it's simply too strong to let others get ahold of.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a teacher excitedly teaching, bundled up in the cold. Snow falling outside. Grumpy children being taught inside.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine the teaching teacher teaching you about Japan in きょうと in winter. You've always wanted to go to きょうと!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Teachers can teach the most about Japan in winter by going to <reading>きょう</reading>と. きょうと is where all the old temples and history of Japan is centered. And it's really gorgeous when it snows.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>teacher</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> is happiest because they get to <kanji>teach</kanji>! The kids can't go out and play because it's too cold. They all have to come in and stay warm as the teacher teaches!

Data.MeaningHint: If a king has no one to rule then he has no reason to exist.
Data.ReadingHint: Just like you can't have a king without a village, you can't have reason without re.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You can't have <kanji>reason</kanji> without <reading>re</reading> (り).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical>'s <radical>village</radical> is the <kanji>reason</kanji> he's king. Without the village there would be no reason to have a king in the first place.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a group of people threaded together by their top hats. Don't you want to be part of a cool group like that? Your own top hat team.
Data.ReadingHint: What do you do in the cumin group? You smell the cumin. Cook with it. Taste it. This group lives and breathes cumin!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Everyone in this <kanji>group</kanji> loves <reading>cumi</reading>n (くみ). You know, the spice or seed. The group take off their top hats and fill them with cumin. You're officially a group for lovers of cumin.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Sew <radical>thread</radical> through your <radical>top hat</radical> and to the top hats of other people you know and you'll turn into a <kanji>group</kanji> of top hat wearing people. You're all connected by the thread now, so everyone will assume you're a group, even if that's all you guys have going for yourselves.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture this boat. It's still a boat, but there is a big mouth on the front and it has fins that it paddles forward with.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture this mouth and boat with a bunch of centaurs riding in it. Centaurs have a lot more parts than humans, so it makes sense that their boats have more parts (animal ones) too.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>boat</kanji> belongs to the <reading>cen</reading>taurs (せん). That's probably why you've never seen a boat like it before.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>boat</radical> has <radical>fins</radical> and a <radical>mouth</radical>! It's still a <kanji>boat</kanji> though.

Data.MeaningHint: A week in earth time is also known as a "full scoot" in scooter lingo. ;)
Data.ReadingHint: You can't ride a scooter for a week barefoot, are you nuts?! Get some shoes!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: If you want to scoot for a <kanji>week</kanji>, you'll need <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). Good shoes too, not sandals or high heels or boots. Solid, high quality shoes, so you don't fall off the scooter and hurt yourself.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Use a <radical>scooter</radical> to travel the <radical>circumference</radical> of the world and it will take you a full <kanji>week</kanji>. Seven whole days!

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine Wolverine in the pouring rain, creating a little halo of snow around him wherever he goes. It's strangely beautiful. That is one cold, traumatized dude…
Data.ReadingHint: Sheesh, emotionally distant people are complicated. The more you keep trying to help them, the more they reject you. Wolverine could do with a nice holiday to melt all that snow away.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You're concerned for Wolverine's mental health. Him being so emotionally cold that he turns rain to <kanji>snow</kanji> means <reading>you kee</reading>p (ゆき) asking if he's okay. But that only makes him want to be more distant.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: When <radical>rain</radical> falls on <radical>Wolverine</radical>, it turns to <kanji>snow</kanji>. He's so emotionally distant and cold that the rain freezes into snow as soon as it gets near him.

Data.MeaningHint: Nothing fishy about that, right?
Data.ReadingHint: Have him continue to scream "GO GYO GYO GYO!" as you try to get your engine started and attempt to flee your impending doom. The fish helps too, because he's pretty worried as well.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You catch a <kanji>fish</kanji>. As you pull it out of the water, it starts yelling and pointing: "<reading>GO</reading>!" (ぎょ). "GO GYO GYO GYO!" There's a huge cruise ship coming right towards your little fishing boat. No wonder the fish was yelling GO at you.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>fish</radical> and the kanji <kanji>fish</kanji> are exactly the same!

Data.MeaningHint: As long as you know the radical bird you know the kanji bird.
Data.ReadingHint: After bursting out of this bird, she turns her head sharply in your direction. "I don't just own the streets, I own the birds too. I'm inside every bird, and I, Mrs. Chou, can burst out at any time."
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Think of a run-of-the-mill <kanji>bird</kanji>, whatever comes to mind for you first is fine. Then, your lovely bird swells. The shape of a face, then a hand, start stretching the bird apart until it explodes outward. Covered in blood and feathers is Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>bird</radical> radical and the <kanji>bird</kanji> kanji are the same!

Data.ReadingHint: The walls are coming in closer, too. Make sure you feel a bit of peril as you look for this key. The walls have a slight opening at the bottom too, so if you don't find it before the wall goes over it, the key will be lost forever, and you'll be smushed!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You're in a room, surrounded completely by <kanji>yellow</kanji>. You can't get out. Somewhere in the room is a yellow <reading>key</reading> (き) but you can't find it, because everything looks the same. You crawl around trying to find this tiny thing so that you can escape.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>yellow</radical> and the kanji <kanji>yellow</kanji> are exactly the same!

Data.ReadingHint: You need to drink all this Coke, otherwise you're going to drown. Drink this Coke and keep tasting its cold sweetness as you drink it all to survive.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You're in a completely <kanji>black</kanji> room. There's no way out, and you can't see. The room starts to fill with liquid. It gets in your mouth. You taste it. It's <reading>Coke</reading> (こく)! Mm, tasty Coke.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>black</radical> and the kanji <kanji>black</kanji> are exactly the same.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a table tilted on its side with some of its legs longer than others, a perfect slide for insects! Imagine all the different insects jumping into the wind each in their own particular style.
Data.ReadingHint: The insects ate all the food that was left on the table, and now they can't just fly away into the wind like they normally do. Instead, they have to try this new style by using the table as a slide to help them take off into the wind.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You wonder what's pushing these insects to jump into the <kanji>wind</kanji> in such a manner, and you realize that it's because they've had way too much <reading>foo</reading>d (ふう).
Data.MeaningMnemonic:
You look at a <radical>table</radical> and notice it is being used as a <radical>slide</radical> by <radical>insect</radical>s to launch themselves into the <kanji>wind</kanji>.

This kanji can also mean <kanji>style</kanji>. To remember this, consider how styles can change quickly, just like the <kanji>wind</kanji>!

Data.MeaningHint: Look around you, everything you see is reflecting the light of the sun. Unless it's night time, then the tiny sun in the center of your lightbulb is creating the light that reflects there.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the light of the sun reflecting off a single acorn on the forest floor. So much symbolism!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>reflect</kanji>ed light is important for <reading>a</reading>corns (えい), in particular. Without light reflecting off everything, the sun's light would never make it down to the ground where they've fallen. They would never grow into trees. And we'd all suffocate.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> in the <radical>center</radical> of the sky gives off its light, which <kanji>reflect</kanji>s off of everything around it.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture the first Japanese person to see an elephant. He stops dead in his tracks, points and screams out, "What a phenomenon!"
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the shougun standing over a pit of zombie elephants. None of these are getting out on his watch, letting people believe in such crazy phenomenons.
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
<kanji>Elephant</kanji>s are great and all, except that when they die they turn into elephant <reading>zo</reading>mbies (ぞう). But you never see these zombies because the <reading>shou</reading>gun (しょう) has a special project in place that destroys all elephant bodies when they die.

Hint: The readings and meanings for this kanji match up really nicely. ぞう is always for elephant and しょう is always for phenomenon. Remember it like this, the elephant is the zombie and the shougun keeps everyone from finding out about this phenomenon.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>elephant</radical> radical and the <kanji>elephant</kanji> kanji are the same. But! This kanji has another meaning: <kanji>phenomenon</kanji>. If you were the first person to see an elephant, you'd call it that too.

Data.MeaningHint: You lay it down, cut it into slats, and voila! A floor! And a bed!
Data.ReadingHint: You can imagine how comfortable it is to sleep on a floor of yucca leaves, but at the same time how horrible the smell must be when they start to rot.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: It's great having a <kanji>floor</kanji> or <kanji>bed</kanji>, but whenever you use it you say <reading>yucka</reading> (ゆか) because it just gets so dirty. That's the one flaw of using a floor as a bed. In fact, it's the dirtiest floor you've ever seen, because it's covered in <reading>yucca</reading> (ゆか) leaves. You're sleeping on a yucca floor. Ew!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Under the <radical>canopy</radical>, you use a <radical>tree</radical> for your <kanji>floor</kanji>. It also becomes your <kanji>bed</kanji>, because hey, what is a floor but the original bed?
